lessons.title: The Transformer Revolution
lessons.explanation: Before Transformers, RNNs processed sequences step-by-step, which was slow. Vaswani et al. showed attention mechanisms alone could match RNN performance while processing entire sequences in parallel on GPUs.

exercises.question: Why is parallel processing important for modern deep learning? A) It makes code simpler B) It enables faster training on GPUs C) It reduces memory usage
exercises.answer: It enables faster training on GPUs
exercises.explanation: Before Transformers, RNNs processed sequences step-by-step, which was slow. Vaswani et al. showed attention mechanisms alone could match RNN performance while processing entire sequences in parallel on GPUs.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- RNNs process sequentially
- Transformers process in parallel
- Attention replaces recurrence

## Explanation

Before Transformers, RNNs processed sequences step-by-step, which was slow. Vaswani et al. showed attention mechanisms alone could match RNN performance while processing entire sequences in parallel on GPUs.

title: Core: How Attention Works
description: The building blocks of Transformer architecture

lessons.title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention
lessons.explanation: The attention formula Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V divides by √d_k to keep softmax gradients stable. Without scaling, large dot products squash gradients to near-zero.

exercises.question: What does the scaling factor √d_k prevent? A) Numerical overflow B) Vanishing gradients in the softmax C) Memory leaks
exercises.explanation: The attention formula Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V divides by √d_k to keep softmax gradients stable. Without scaling, large dot products squash gradients to near-zero.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- Queries match Keys
- Values weighted by scores
- Scaling prevents vanishing gradients

## Explanation

The attention formula Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V divides by √d_k to keep softmax gradients stable. Without scaling, large dot products squash gradients to near-zero.

lessons.title: Multi-Head Attention: Seeing Multiple Perspectives
lessons.explanation: Multi-head attention runs 8 independent attention mechanisms in parallel, each learning to focus on different aspects (e.g., one head for grammar, another for semantics). Results are concatenated.

exercises.question: Why use 8 attention heads instead of one? A) Faster computation B) Learn attention from different subspaces simultaneously C) Reduce memory
exercises.answer: Learn attention from different subspaces simultaneously
exercises.explanation: Multi-head attention runs 8 independent attention mechanisms in parallel, each learning to focus on different aspects (e.g., one head for grammar, another for semantics). Results are concatenated.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- 8 parallel attention heads
- Different representation subspaces
- Attention from multiple viewpoints

## Explanation

Multi-head attention runs 8 independent attention mechanisms in parallel, each learning to focus on different aspects (e.g., one head for grammar, another for semantics). Results are concatenated.

lessons.title: Encoder-Decoder Architecture
lessons.explanation: The encoder processes input and outputs representations. The decoder attends to these encoder outputs via cross-attention while generating output token-by-token, combining self-attention (to itself) with encoder context.

exercises.question: What does the decoder's cross-attention layer do? A) Attends to itself B) Attends to encoder outputs C) Generates new embeddings
exercises.explanation: The encoder processes input and outputs representations. The decoder attends to these encoder outputs via cross-attention while generating output token-by-token, combining self-attention (to itself) with encoder context.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- 6 encoder layers
- 6 decoder layers
- Cross-attention between them

## Explanation

The encoder processes input and outputs representations. The decoder attends to these encoder outputs via cross-attention while generating output token-by-token, combining self-attention (to itself) with encoder context.

lessons.title: Positional Encoding: Adding Sequence Order
lessons.explanation: Unlike RNNs that process left-to-right, attention treats all positions equally. Sinusoidal positional encodings (sin/cos waves at different frequencies) inject position information before attention computes relationships.

exercises.question: Why do Transformers need positional encoding? A) Attention has no inherent sequence order B) To speed up training C) To reduce embeddings size
exercises.answer: Attention has no inherent sequence order
exercises.explanation: Unlike RNNs that process left-to-right, attention treats all positions equally. Sinusoidal positional encodings (sin/cos waves at different frequencies) inject position information before attention computes relationships.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- Sinusoidal functions
- No learned parameters
- Encodes absolute position

## Explanation

Unlike RNNs that process left-to-right, attention treats all positions equally. Sinusoidal positional encodings (sin/cos waves at different frequencies) inject position information before attention computes relationships.

lessons.title: Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks
lessons.explanation: Each layer includes an MLP that expands attention outputs to 2048 dimensions (adding capacity), applies ReLU nonlinearity, then contracts back to 512. This adds expressivity beyond attention.

exercises.question: What is the purpose of expanding to 2048 dimensions then back to 512? A) Reduce computation B) Add nonlinearity and expressivity C) Match GPU memory
exercises.explanation: Each layer includes an MLP that expands attention outputs to 2048 dimensions (adding capacity), applies ReLU nonlinearity, then contracts back to 512. This adds expressivity beyond attention.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- Two-layer MLP per position
- 512→2048→512 expansion
- ReLU nonlinearity

## Explanation

Each layer includes an MLP that expands attention outputs to 2048 dimensions (adding capacity), applies ReLU nonlinearity, then contracts back to 512. This adds expressivity beyond attention.

lessons.title: Self-Attention: Sequence Attends to Itself
key_ideas: Positions attend to all other positions
lessons.explanation: Self-attention computes how much each position should attend to every other position. For 10 tokens, all 10×10=100 pairwise relationships are computed in parallel, enabling the model to directly relate distant words.

exercises.question: How many relationships does self-attention compute for a 10-token sequence? A) 10 B) 100 C) 1024
exercises.explanation: Self-attention computes how much each position should attend to every other position. For 10 tokens, all 10×10=100 pairwise relationships are computed in parallel, enabling the model to directly relate distant words.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- Positions attend to all other positions
- Computes all relationships in parallel
- Core of encoder

## Explanation

Self-attention computes how much each position should attend to every other position. For 10 tokens, all 10×10=100 pairwise relationships are computed in parallel, enabling the model to directly relate distant words.

lessons.title: Causal Masking: Preventing Future Cheating
lessons.explanation: During training, the decoder must not see future ground truth tokens. Causal masking sets future attention weights to -∞ before softmax, forcing attention to zero. This enables autoregressive generation at test time.

exercises.question: Why mask future tokens in the decoder? A) Save computation B) Prevent the decoder from 'cheating' by looking ahead C) Increase accuracy
exercises.answer: Prevent the decoder from 'cheating' by looking ahead
exercises.explanation: During training, the decoder must not see future ground truth tokens. Causal masking sets future attention weights to -∞ before softmax, forcing attention to zero. This enables autoregressive generation at test time.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- Mask future tokens
- Prevents decoder from seeing output
- Enables autoregressive generation

## Explanation

During training, the decoder must not see future ground truth tokens. Causal masking sets future attention weights to -∞ before softmax, forcing attention to zero. This enables autoregressive generation at test time.

lessons.title: Training the Transformer
lessons.explanation: Vaswani et al. use Adam optimizer with a custom schedule: linearly increase learning rate for 4000 warmup steps, then decay by steps^-0.5. Dropout (0.1) and label smoothing (ε=0.1) prevent overfitting. 12 hours on 8 P100 GPUs trains the base model.

exercises.question: What does the warmup schedule do? A) Initialize weights warmly B) Gradually increase learning rate for 4000 steps C) Reduce training time
exercises.answer: Gradually increase learning rate for 4000 steps
exercises.explanation: Vaswani et al. use Adam optimizer with a custom schedule: linearly increase learning rate for 4000 warmup steps, then decay by steps^-0.5. Dropout (0.1) and label smoothing (ε=0.1) prevent overfitting. 12 hours on 8 P100 GPUs trains the base model.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- Warmup schedule
- Adam optimizer
- Dropout and label smoothing

## Explanation

Vaswani et al. use Adam optimizer with a custom schedule: linearly increase learning rate for 4000 warmup steps, then decay by steps^-0.5. Dropout (0.1) and label smoothing (ε=0.1) prevent overfitting. 12 hours on 8 P100 GPUs trains the base model.

lessons.title: Results: Machine Translation Excellence
lessons.explanation: The Transformer achieves state-of-the-art 28.4 BLEU (English-German) and 41.8 BLEU (English-French) on WMT 2014, training in just 3.5 days on 8 GPUs — much faster than previous RNN-based approaches.

exercises.question: What is the primary advantage of Transformer over RNNs? A) Higher accuracy only B) Faster training while maintaining accuracy C) Smaller model size
exercises.explanation: The Transformer achieves state-of-the-art 28.4 BLEU (English-German) and 41.8 BLEU (English-French) on WMT 2014, training in just 3.5 days on 8 GPUs — much faster than previous RNN-based approaches.

lessons.content:
## Key Ideas

- 28.4 BLEU on EN-DE
- 41.8 BLEU on EN-FR
- 3.5x faster training

## Explanation

The Transformer achieves state-of-the-art 28.4 BLEU (English-German) and 41.8 BLEU (English-French) on WMT 2014, training in just 3.5 days on 8 GPUs — much faster than previous RNN-based approaches.
